preamble.summary: Psalm 44 is a national lament of the Sons of Korah, spoken by the community after a devastating military defeat. The psalm moves through four distinct movements: remembrance of God's past saving acts in the conquest (vv. 2-9), the present crisis of defeat and disgrace (vv. 10-17), a protestation of innocence — the community has not broken covenant (vv. 18-23), and a desperate plea for God to awaken and act (vv. 24-27). The psalm's theological nerve is its insistence that the defeat cannot be explained by Israel's unfaithfulness.
preamble.remarkable: Psalm 44 is one of the boldest texts in the Hebrew Bible because it directly confronts the retribution principle — the theology that suffering results from sin. The community insists: 'All this has come upon us, yet we have not forgotten you, and we have not been false to your covenant' (v. 18). This is not Job's individual protest but an entire community saying to God: we did not break faith, and you still let us be slaughtered. Verse 23 — 'For your sake we are killed all day long; we are counted as sheep for slaughter' — will be quoted by Paul in Romans 8:36 to argue that suffering does not separate believers from God's love. The psalm's final cry, 'Awake! Why do you sleep, O Lord?' (v. 24), is extraordinary: it attributes sleep to the Almighty, echoing and reversing Elijah's mockery of Baal on Carmel (1 Kings 18:27).
preamble.friction: The claim of innocence in verses 18-23 has troubled interpreters. Is the community genuinely innocent, or is this rhetorical exaggeration? The psalm leaves no room for ambiguity — the community explicitly denies covenant violation. This creates a theological crisis: if God is just and Israel is faithful, why the defeat? The psalm offers no answer; it simply holds the contradiction before God and demands a response. The phrase be-kol yom ('all day long') in verse 23 intensifies the suffering into a permanent condition. The historical setting is debated — some place it in the Assyrian period, others during the Maccabean crisis, but the psalm's refusal to name the enemy makes it available for any generation's suffering.
preamble.connections: The protest of innocent suffering links this psalm to Job (especially Job 9:22-24, where Job accuses God of destroying the innocent alongside the wicked). The 'sheep for slaughter' image (v. 23) connects to Isaiah 53:7 (the suffering servant led like a lamb to slaughter). Paul's quotation in Romans 8:36 recontextualizes the verse: suffering is real but does not indicate divine abandonment. The plea for God to 'awake' (v. 24) inverts the mockery of 1 Kings 18:27, where Elijah tells Baal worshipers their god is sleeping. Here Israel dares to say the same to the LORD.

verses.text_hebrew: לַמְנַצֵּ֬חַ לִבְנֵי־קֹ֬רַח מַשְׂכִּֽיל׃
verses.text_kjv: To the chief Musician for the sons of Korah, Maschil.
verses.rendering: For the choirmaster. A maskil of the Sons of Korah.
translator_notes: The maskil designation and Korahite attribution continue from Psalm 42. As a communal lament, this psalm would have been performed liturgically — the Sons of Korah are not merely named as authors but as the performing guild who gave voice to the nation's grief.

verses.text_hebrew: אֱלֹהִ֤ים ׀ בְּאׇזְנֵ֬ינוּ שָׁמַ֗עְנוּ אֲבוֹתֵ֥ינוּ סִפְּרוּ־לָ֑נוּ פֹּ֥עַל פָּעַ֥לְתָּ בִ֝ימֵיהֶ֗ם בִּ֣ימֵי קֶֽדֶם׃
verses.text_kjv: We have heard with our ears, O God, our fathers have told us, what work thou didst in their days, in the times of old.
verses.rendering:
O God, with our ears we have heard —
our ancestors told us —
the deeds you performed in their days,
in the days of old.
translator_notes: The psalm begins with oral tradition: be-oznenu shama'nu ('with our ears we have heard'). This is faith received by testimony, passed from generation to generation. The emphasis on hearing (not reading) places the community in the living chain of witness. The deeds (po'al) refer to the conquest and deliverance narratives. The appeal to 'days of old' (yeme qedem) establishes the contrast that drives the psalm: God acted then; why not now?

verses.text_hebrew: אַתָּ֤ה ׀ יָדְךָ֗ גּוֹיִ֥ם הוֹרַ֗שְׁתָּ וַתִּטָּעֵ֑ם תָּרַ֥ע לְ֝אֻמִּ֗ים וַתְּשַׁלְּחֵֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: How thou didst drive out the heathen with thy hand, and plantedst them; how thou didst afflict the people, and cast them out.
verses.rendering:
You — your hand drove out nations and planted our ancestors;
you shattered peoples and spread them out.
translator_notes: The agricultural metaphor is vivid: God uprooted nations (horashta, from yarash, 'to dispossess, to drive out') and planted (vattitta'em) Israel in their place. Israel is a vine planted in cleared land. The verb tara ('you shattered, you broke') is severe — God broke the peoples already in the land. The verbs alternate between planting Israel and removing the nations, creating a rhythmic pattern of displacement and establishment.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּ֤י לֹ֪א בְחַרְבָּ֡ם יָ֥רְשׁוּ אָ֗רֶץ וּזְרוֹעָ֥ם לֹא־הוֹשִׁ֣יעָה לָּ֑מוֹ כִּֽי־יְמִ֖ינְךָ֣ וּזְרוֹעֲךָ֮ וְא֥וֹר פָּ֝נֶ֗יךָ כִּ֣י רְצִיתָֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: For they got not the land in possession by their own sword, neither did their own arm save them: but thy right hand, and thine arm, and the light of thy countenance, because thou hadst a favour unto them.
verses.rendering:
For not by their own sword did they take the land,
and their own arm did not save them;
but your right hand and your arm
and the light of your face — because you delighted in them.
verses.expanded_rendering: The theological principle is stated with force: Israel's conquest was not achieved by military prowess. lo be-charbam ('not by their sword') and uzro'am lo hoshi'ah lamo ('and their arm did not save them') — human weapons and human strength are explicitly denied credit. What won the land was yeminekha uzro'akha ve-or panekha ('your right hand and your arm and the light of your face'). God's right hand (yemin) signifies power and favor; God's arm (zero'a) signifies strength in action; the light of God's face (or panekha) signifies presence and pleasure. The reason: ki retsitam ('because you delighted in them'). The conquest happened because of divine favor, not human merit. This theological premise is the foundation for the protest that follows: if victory came from you, then defeat must also come from you.
translator_notes: The phrase or panekha ('the light of your face') connects to the priestly blessing of Numbers 6:25 ('the LORD make his face shine upon you'). The shining face of God is the visible expression of divine favor — when it is present, victory follows; when it is withdrawn, defeat comes.

verses.text_hebrew: אַתָּה־ה֣וּא מַלְכִּ֣י אֱלֹהִ֑ים צַ֝וֵּ֗ה יְשׁוּע֥וֹת יַעֲקֹֽב׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou art my King, O God: command deliverances for Jacob.
verses.rendering:
You are my King, O God;
command victories for Jacob.
translator_notes: The singular malki ('my King') within a communal psalm may indicate a worship leader speaking on behalf of the congregation. The imperative tsavveh ('command!') treats God as a military commander who can order victories as a king dispatches troops. yeshu'ot Ya'aqov ('victories/salvations of Jacob') uses the plural of yeshu'ah, suggesting repeated or comprehensive deliverance.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּ֭ךָ צָרֵ֣ינוּ נְנַגֵּ֑חַ בְּ֝שִׁמְךָ֗ נָב֥וּס קָמֵֽינוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Through thee will we push down our enemies: through thy name will we tread them under that rise up against us.
verses.rendering:
Through you we gore our adversaries;
through your name we trample those who rise against us.
translator_notes: The verb nenaggeach ('we gore') is a bull-fighting image — the horned animal thrusting its enemies. This is raw, physical language for military victory. The parallel be-shimkha ('through your name') means through the invocation of God's name in battle — the war cry that carries divine authority.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּ֤י לֹ֣א בְקַשְׁתִּ֣י אֶבְטָ֑ח וְ֝חַרְבִּ֗י לֹ֣א תוֹשִׁיעֵֽנִי׃
verses.text_kjv: For I will not trust in my bow, neither shall my sword save me.
verses.rendering:
For I do not trust in my bow,
and my sword cannot save me.
translator_notes: The confession lo be-qashti evtach ('not in my bow do I trust') uses the same verb batach ('to trust') that the psalms repeatedly reserve for confidence in God. Placing trust in weapons is the opposite of placing trust in God. The community explicitly disowns military self-reliance.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּ֤י הוֹשַׁעְתָּ֣נוּ מִצָּרֵ֑ינוּ וּ֝מְשַׂנְאֵ֗ינוּ הֱבִישֽׁוֹתָ׃
verses.text_kjv: But thou hast saved us from our enemies, and hast put them to shame that hated us.
verses.rendering:
For you have saved us from our adversaries
and put to shame those who hate us.
translator_notes: The perfect tense hoshata'nu ('you have saved us') recalls past deliverances. The verb hevishota ('you have put to shame') from bosh indicates public disgrace — God caused the enemies to suffer the humiliation they intended for Israel.

verses.text_hebrew: בֵּאלֹהִ֣ים הִלַּ֣לְנוּ כׇל־הַיּ֑וֹם וְשִׁמְךָ֓ ׀ לְעוֹלָ֖ם נוֹדֶ֣ה סֶֽלָה׃
verses.text_kjv: In God we boast all the day long, and praise thy name for ever. Selah.
verses.rendering:
In God we have gloried all day long,
and your name we will thank forever. Selah.
translator_notes: hillalnu ('we have gloried, we have praised') from halal is the root of 'hallelujah.' The community's boasting is in God, not in themselves — this prepares for the devastating reversal in the next verse. The selah marks a musical pause or interlude, allowing the contrast between past glory and present disaster to sink in.

verses.text_hebrew: אַף־זָנַ֥חְתָּ וַתַּכְלִימֵ֑נוּ וְלֹא־תֵ֝צֵ֗א בְּצִבְאוֹתֵֽינוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: But thou hast cast off, and put us to shame; and goest not forth with our armies.
verses.rendering:
Yet you have rejected us and humiliated us
and do not go out with our armies.
translator_notes: The hinge word af ('yet, but, indeed') marks the devastating turn. zanachta ('you have rejected') and vatakhliemnu ('you have humiliated us') use the same verbs previously applied to enemies — now God does to Israel what he once did to their foes. The phrase lo tetse be-tsiv'otenu ('you do not go out with our armies') is the core accusation: God has withdrawn from battle. In Israelite warfare theology, God marched with the army (cf. Deuteronomy 20:4). His absence means certain defeat.

verses.text_hebrew: תְּשִׁיבֵ֥נוּ אָח֗וֹר מִנִּי־צָ֑ר וּ֝מְשַׂנְאֵ֗ינוּ שָׁ֣סוּ לָֽמוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou makest us to turn back from the enemy: and they which hate us spoil for themselves.
verses.rendering:
You make us retreat before the foe,
and those who hate us plunder at will.
translator_notes: Verse 11 continues the psalm's thematic and structural development.

verses.text_hebrew: תִּתְּנֵ֣נוּ כְּ֭צֹאן מַאֲכָ֑ל וּ֝בַגּוֹיִ֗ם זֵרִיתָֽנוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou hast given us like sheep appointed for meat; and hast scattered us among the heathen.
verses.rendering:
You hand us over like sheep for slaughter
and scatter us among the nations.
translator_notes: tson ma'akhal ('sheep for eating/slaughter') is an image of helpless livestock led to death. The verb zeritanu ('you have scattered us') from zarah ('to scatter, to winnow') suggests exile — dispersion among the nations like grain flung into the wind.

verses.text_hebrew: תִּמְכֹּר־עַמְּךָ֥ בְלֹא־ה֑וֹן וְלֹ֥א רִ֝בִּ֗יתָ בִּמְחִירֵיהֶֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou sellest thy people for nought, and dost not increase thy wealth by their price.
verses.rendering:
You sell your people for nothing
and set no high price on them.
verses.expanded_rendering: The accusation intensifies with commercial metaphor. timkor ammekha be-lo hon ('you sell your people for no wealth') — God has sold Israel like merchandise, but not even for a good price. lo ribbita bimchirehem ('you did not profit from their price') uses the language of profit and increase. The insult is double: not only has God given his people away, he gave them away cheaply, as though they were worthless. This is not theological abstraction but the raw language of a community that feels discarded by its own God.
translator_notes: The commercial metaphor of God 'selling' his people appears also in Judges 2:14, 3:8, and 4:2, where God 'sells' Israel into the hands of oppressors as punishment for idolatry. But here, the community insists they have not sinned (vv. 18-22) — so the sale is unjust, or at least inexplicable.

verses.text_hebrew: תְּשִׂימֵ֣נוּ חֶ֭רְפָּה לִשְׁכֵנֵ֑ינוּ לַ֥עַג וָ֝קֶ֗לֶס לִסְבִיבוֹתֵֽינוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou makest us a reproach to our neighbours, a scorn and a derision to them that are round about us.
verses.rendering:
You make us a taunt to our neighbors,
a mockery and derision to those around us.
translator_notes: Verse 14 continues the psalm's thematic and structural development.

verses.text_hebrew: תְּשִׂימֵ֣נוּ מָ֭שָׁל בַּגּוֹיִ֑ם מְנוֹד־רֹ֝֗אשׁ בַּלְאֻמִּֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou makest us a byword among the heathen, a shaking of the head among the people.
verses.rendering:
You make us a byword among the nations,
a shaking of the head among the peoples.
translator_notes: mashal ('byword, proverb') means Israel's name has become a cautionary tale — other nations use Israel as an example of ruin. menod rosh ('shaking of the head') is the gesture of contemptuous pity — people look at Israel and shake their heads in mockery.

verses.text_hebrew: כׇּל־הַ֭יּוֹם כְּלִמָּתִ֣י נֶגְדִּ֑י וּבֹ֖שֶׁת פָּנַ֣י כִּסָּֽתְנִי׃
verses.text_kjv: My confusion is continually before me, and the shame of my face hath covered me,
verses.rendering:
All day long my disgrace is before me,
and shame covers my face,
translator_notes: Verse 16 continues the psalm's thematic and structural development.

verses.text_hebrew: מִ֭קּוֹל מְחָרֵ֣ף וּמְגַדֵּ֑ף מִפְּנֵ֖י א֣וֹיֵב וּמִתְנַקֵּֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: For the voice of him that reproacheth and blasphemeth; by reason of the enemy and avenger.
verses.rendering:
because of the voice of the one who taunts and reviles,
because of the enemy and the avenger.
translator_notes: Verse 17 continues the psalm's thematic and structural development.

verses.text_hebrew: כׇּל־זֹ֣את בָּ֭אַתְנוּ וְלֹ֣א שְׁכַחֲנ֑וּךָ וְלֹֽא־שִׁ֝קַּ֗רְנוּ בִּבְרִיתֶֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: All this is come upon us; yet have we not forgotten thee, neither have we dealt falsely in thy covenant.
verses.rendering:
All this has come upon us,
yet we have not forgotten you
and we have not been false to your covenant.
verses.expanded_rendering: This is the psalm's theological earthquake. kol zot ba'atnu ('all this has come upon us') acknowledges the full weight of disaster. But lo shekhachunukha ('we have not forgotten you') denies the most common explanation for suffering — that Israel forgot God, that they turned to idols, that they broke faith. The second denial ve-lo shiqqarnu biveritekha ('and we have not been false to your covenant') is even more specific: the community has not violated the covenant terms. The verb shiqqer ('to deal falsely, to lie') implies deliberate treachery. The community stands before God and declares: we kept our end of the covenant; you did not keep yours. This is not blasphemy but the most honest form of faith — holding God accountable to God's own promises.
translator_notes: The insistence on innocence distinguishes Psalm 44 from most other national laments, which typically include confession of sin (cf. Psalm 106, Daniel 9, Nehemiah 9). This community does not confess; it protests. The theological problem this creates — why does a faithful people suffer? — is left unresolved within the psalm itself.

key_terms.semantic_range: covenant, treaty, alliance, binding agreement, pact
key_terms.note: berit is the foundational concept of Israel's relationship with God — a binding commitment with mutual obligations. To be false (shiqqer) to the covenant is to violate its terms. The community's claim is that they have fulfilled their covenantal obligations, making their suffering inexplicable within the retribution framework.

verses.text_hebrew: לֹא־נָס֣וֹג אָח֣וֹר לִבֵּ֑נוּ וַתֵּ֥ט אֲ֝שֻׁרֵ֗ינוּ מִנִּ֥י אׇרְחֶֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Our heart is not turned back, neither have our steps declined from thy way;
verses.rendering:
Our heart has not turned back,
nor have our steps strayed from your path,
translator_notes: Verse 19 continues the psalm's thematic and structural development.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּ֤י דִכִּיתָ֣נוּ בִמְק֣וֹם תַּנִּ֑ים וַתְּכַ֖ס עָלֵ֣ינוּ בְצַלְמָֽוֶת׃
verses.text_kjv: Though thou hast sore broken us in the place of dragons, and covered us with the shadow of death.
verses.rendering:
yet you have crushed us in the haunt of jackals
and covered us with deep darkness.
translator_notes: meqom tannim ('place of jackals') suggests desolate ruins — jackals inhabit abandoned places. tsalmavet ('deep darkness, shadow of death') is a compound word debated among scholars. It may derive from tsel ('shadow') + mavet ('death') or from tsalmut ('deep darkness'). Either way, it describes the most extreme darkness imaginable — the darkness of Sheol, of death, of total divine absence.

key_terms.semantic_range: shadow of death, deep darkness, utter gloom, the darkness of Sheol
key_terms.note: tsalmavet is the same word used in Psalm 23:4 ('the valley of tsalmavet'). Whether parsed as 'shadow of death' or 'deep darkness,' it represents the extreme of human suffering — the felt experience of death's proximity and God's absence.

verses.text_hebrew: אִם־שָׁ֭כַחְנוּ שֵׁ֣ם אֱלֹהֵ֑ינוּ וַנִּפְרֹ֥שׂ כַּ֝פֵּ֗ינוּ לְאֵ֣ל זָֽר׃
verses.text_kjv: If we have forgotten the name of our God, or stretched out our hands to a strange god;
verses.rendering:
If we had forgotten the name of our God
or spread out our hands to a foreign god,
translator_notes: Verse 21 continues the psalm's thematic and structural development.

verses.text_hebrew: הֲלֹ֣א אֱ֭לֹהִים יַֽחֲקׇר־זֹ֑את כִּֽי־ה֥וּא יֹ֝דֵ֗עַ תַּעֲלֻמ֥וֹת לֵֽב׃
verses.text_kjv: Shall not God search this out? for he knoweth the secrets of the heart.
verses.rendering:
would not God search this out?
For he knows the secrets of the heart.
translator_notes: The community appeals to divine omniscience as their witness. ta'alumot lev ('secrets of the heart') — if they had worshiped foreign gods even in thought, God would know. Their appeal to God's knowledge of hidden things is itself an act of radical transparency: they invite investigation and declare confidence in the outcome.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּֽי־עָ֭לֶיךָ הֹרַ֣גְנוּ כׇל־הַיּ֑וֹם נֶ֝חְשַׁ֗בְנוּ כְּצֹ֣אן טִבְחָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: Yea, for thy sake are we killed all the day long; we are counted as sheep for the slaughter.
verses.rendering:
For your sake we are killed all day long;
we are counted as sheep for slaughter.
verses.expanded_rendering: The most famous verse of the psalm. ki alekha horagnu kol hayyom ('for on account of you we are killed all day long') makes an extraordinary claim: the community's suffering is not punishment for sin but the cost of faithfulness to God. They die because they are God's people, because they bear God's name. nechshavnu ke-tson tivchah ('we are reckoned as sheep for slaughter') — the verb chashav ('to reckon, to count, to think') means they are categorized, classified, treated as animals destined for killing. The image strips them of human dignity — they are not warriors falling in battle but livestock being processed. Paul quotes this verse in Romans 8:36 and then immediately responds: 'In all these things we are more than conquerors through the one who loved us.'
translator_notes: The phrase ki alekha ('for your sake, on account of you') is theologically central. The suffering is not random, not punitive, but vocational — it is the consequence of belonging to God in a hostile world. This reading anticipates the Christian theology of suffering for the sake of Christ and the Jewish theology of kiddush hashem (sanctification of the divine name through martyrdom).

verses.text_hebrew: ע֤וּרָה ׀ לָ֣מָּה תִישַׁ֣ן ׀ אֲדֹנָ֑י הָ֝קִ֗יצָה אַל־תִּזְנַ֥ח לָנֶֽצַח׃
verses.text_kjv: Awake, why sleepest thou, O Lord? arise, cast us not off for ever.
verses.rendering:
Awake! Why do you sleep, O Lord?
Rise up! Do not reject us forever.
verses.expanded_rendering: The boldest imperative in the Psalter. urah lammah tishan Adonai ('awake — why do you sleep, Lord?') attributes unconsciousness to God. The verb tishan ('you sleep') is not metaphorical hedging — it is a direct accusation that God is asleep at the wheel, oblivious to his people's destruction. haqitsah ('wake up, arise') is the urgent command to shake off sleep. The community dares to say to the Almighty what Elijah mocked Baal's prophets for saying to their god: maybe he is sleeping (1 Kings 18:27). But there is a crucial difference: Elijah mocked a false god; this community challenges the true God. The plea al tiznach lanestsach ('do not reject forever') introduces the terrifying possibility that God's rejection could be permanent — the word netsach ('perpetuity, forever') raises the stakes to cosmic levels.
translator_notes: Psalm 121:4 declares that 'the Guardian of Israel neither slumbers nor sleeps.' Psalm 44:24 takes that theological conviction and throws it back at God as a question: if you do not sleep, then explain why you are acting as though you do. The tension between these two psalms is not resolved — it is held in the canon as a witness to the full range of faithful prayer.

verses.text_hebrew: לָמָּה־פָנֶ֥יךָ תַסְתִּ֑יר תִּשְׁכַּ֖ח עׇנְיֵ֣נוּ וְלַחֲצֵֽנוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Wherefore hidest thou thy face, and forgettest our affliction and our oppression?
verses.rendering:
Why do you hide your face?
Why forget our affliction and our oppression?
translator_notes: The hidden face (hester panim) is the opposite of the shining face (or panim) of verse 4. When God hides his face, favor is withdrawn and disaster follows. onyenu ve-lachatsenu ('our affliction and our oppression') — oni is suffering imposed from outside, lachats is pressure or crushing force. Both words carry the memory of Egypt (Exodus 3:9 uses both terms for Israel's condition under Pharaoh).

verses.text_hebrew: כִּ֤י שָׁ֣חָה לֶעָפָ֣ר נַפְשֵׁ֑נוּ דָּבְקָ֖ה לָאָ֣רֶץ בִּטְנֵֽנוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: For our soul is bowed down to the dust: our belly cleaveth unto the earth.
verses.rendering:
For our soul is bowed down to the dust;
our belly clings to the ground.
translator_notes: The image is prostration — not worship but collapse. shachah le-afar nafshenu ('our soul bows to the dust') and davqah la-arets bitnenu ('our belly sticks to the earth') describe a people face-down in the dirt, too broken to rise. The verb davaq ('to cling, to stick') is the same word used for marriage in Genesis 2:24 and for covenant loyalty — here it describes a body glued to the ground by despair.

verses.text_hebrew: ק֭וּמָה עֶזְרָ֣תָה לָּ֑נוּ וּ֝פְדֵ֗נוּ לְמַ֣עַן חַסְדֶּֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Arise for our help, and redeem us for thy mercies' sake.
verses.rendering:
Rise up — come to our help!
Redeem us for the sake of your faithful love.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm's final cry: qumah ezratah lanu ('rise, be a help to us'). The imperative qumah ('arise, get up') continues the wake-up call of verse 24. The plea u-fedenu le-ma'an chasdekha ('and redeem us for the sake of your faithful love') makes the ultimate appeal — not to Israel's merit (the community has already claimed innocence) but to God's own character. chesed is the ground of the petition: God should act because faithful love is who God is. The verb padah ('to redeem, to ransom') means to buy back, to liberate from bondage or death. The psalm does not end with resolution or praise — it ends with a cry. The silence that follows is the reader's to fill.
translator_notes: The appeal le-ma'an chasdekha ('for the sake of your faithful love') is the psalm's last word on the subject. Having exhausted every argument — past faithfulness, present innocence, the absurdity of God sleeping — the community falls back on the one thing that cannot fail: God's own covenant character. If chesed means anything, it means God cannot abandon his people permanently.

key_terms.semantic_range: loyal love, covenant faithfulness, lovingkindness, steadfast love, mercy, devotion
key_terms.note: chesed is the covenantal attribute the psalmist stakes everything on. If God's chesed is real, then the present suffering cannot be the final word. The psalm ends by placing the burden of response on God's own character.
